Back-Office Technology Functions

CIO Operating Model

Capability Taxonomy

Risk · Finance · Treasury · Controls
Draft v0.1 — For Stakeholder Discussion
DRAFT — For DiscussionCIO Operating Model — Capability Taxonomy1 / 8
Context

Why a Common Capability Taxonomy?

⚡The Capacity Challenge

Regulatory and compliance-driven change consumes a disproportionate share of delivery capacity, limiting strategic investment and innovation.

🤖AI as a Lever

AI adoption is moving from experimentation to scale. A structured view of capabilities helps identify where AI can create the most capacity.

🔄Consistency Across Functions

A shared language enables comparison across CIO functions — identifying where practices can be shared and where bespoke approaches are justified.

📊Peer Benchmarking

A common taxonomy provides the structure to benchmark against competing universal banks and identify areas of relative strength or gap.

DRAFT — For DiscussionCIO Operating Model — Capability Taxonomy2 / 8
Overview

10 Capability Domains · ~50 Capabilities

📊
Demand & Portfolio Management
6 capabilities
🏗️
Solution Design & Architecture
5 capabilities
⚙️
Software Engineering & Delivery
7 capabilities
🖥️
Production Operations & Support
7 capabilities
📈
Data Management & Analytics
5 capabilities
☁️
Infrastructure & Cloud
4 capabilities
🛡️
Security, Risk & Compliance
5 capabilities
👥
Workforce, Talent & Sourcing
5 capabilities
🤖
AI & Intelligent Automation
5 capabilities
📋
Governance, Standards & Improvement
5 capabilities
Change — Domains 1–3
Run — Domains 4–6
Govern — Domains 7 & 10
Enable — Domains 8–9
DRAFT — For DiscussionCIO Operating Model — Capability Taxonomy3 / 8
Capability Details

Change — Demand, Architecture & Delivery

📊1. Demand & Portfolio Mgmt
1.1Demand Intake & Classification
1.2Portfolio Prioritisation & Sequencing
1.3Investment & Funding Management
1.4Capacity Planning & Allocation
1.5Portfolio Governance & Reporting
1.6Dependency Management
🏗️2. Solution Design & Architecture
2.1Enterprise & Domain Architecture
2.2Solution Design
2.3Architecture Governance & Review
2.4Technology Strategy & Lifecycle
2.5Data Architecture & Modelling
⚙️3. Software Engineering & Delivery
3.1Requirements & Analysis
3.2Software Development
3.3Testing & Quality Assurance
3.4Release & Deployment Management
3.5Environment Management
3.6DevOps & CI/CD
3.7Delivery Methodology & Practices
DRAFT — For DiscussionCIO Operating Model — Capability Taxonomy4 / 8
Capability Details

Run — Operations, Data & Infrastructure

🖥️4. Production Operations & Support
4.1Incident Management
4.2Problem Management
4.3Service Monitoring & Observability
4.4Change & Configuration Management
4.5Batch & Job Scheduling
4.6DR & Business Continuity
4.7Production Support (L1/L2/L3)
📈5. Data Management & Analytics
5.1Data Governance & Quality
5.2Data Integration & ETL
5.3Reporting & MI
5.4Data Platform & Infrastructure
5.5Advanced Analytics & AI/ML
☁️6. Infrastructure & Cloud
6.1Cloud Platform Management
6.2Compute & Storage
6.3Network & Connectivity
6.4Infrastructure Automation
DRAFT — For DiscussionCIO Operating Model — Capability Taxonomy5 / 8
Capability Details

Govern & Enable — Security, Workforce, AI & Governance

🛡️7. Security, Risk & Compliance
7.1Technology Risk Mgmt
7.2Cyber Security Ops
7.3Regulatory & Compliance Mgmt
7.4Controls Assurance & Testing
7.5Third-Party & Vendor Risk
👥8. Workforce, Talent & Sourcing
8.1Workforce Planning & Design
8.2Sourcing Strategy & Model
8.3Vendor & SI Management
8.4Skills & Capability Development
8.5Contingent Workforce Mgmt
🤖9. AI & Intelligent Automation
9.1AI-Assisted Development
9.2Intelligent Automation (RPA+)
9.3AI for Operations (AIOps)
9.4AI Governance & Responsible Use
9.5AI Enablement & Experimentation
📋10. Governance & Improvement
10.1Operating Model Governance
10.2Standards & Policies
10.3Metrics & Performance Mgmt
10.4Continuous Improvement
10.5Knowledge Management
DRAFT — For DiscussionCIO Operating Model — Capability Taxonomy6 / 8
Application

How the Taxonomy Will Be Used

01
Current State Baseline
●Maturity assessment per CIO function
●Capacity & cost mapping (change / run / support)
●Sourcing profile per capability
02
AI Opportunity Mapping
●Automation potential per capability
●Effort/complexity scoring
●Prioritised use case pipeline by horizon
03
Peer Benchmarking
●Structured comparison vs. competing banks
●Identify leading practices
●Sourcing model comparison
04
Target Operating Model (1/3/5yr)
●Future-state maturity targets
●Workforce & sourcing evolution
●Capacity reallocation roadmap
DRAFT — For DiscussionCIO Operating Model — Capability Taxonomy7 / 8
Next Steps

Proposed Path Forward

Week 1–2Taxonomy Validation
Working sessions with CIO representatives to validate domains, capabilities, and language. Identify any gaps specific to the bank's context.
Week 2–3Maturity Model Design
Define maturity levels (1–5) for each capability. Establish assessment criteria and evidence requirements.
Week 3–5Current State Assessment
Conduct structured assessments across each CIO function. Map capacity, cost, and sourcing against the taxonomy.
Week 4–6AI Opportunity Mapping & Benchmarking
Assess AI/automation potential per capability. Initiate peer benchmarking against the common taxonomy.
Week 6–8Target Operating Model — Draft
Define 1, 3, and 5-year target states. Articulate the capacity creation roadmap and investment case.
DRAFT — For DiscussionCIO Operating Model — Capability Taxonomy8 / 8
